link|Q24865035}} [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|馬見岡綿向神社|version=105814433|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{神社 |名称= 馬見岡綿向神社 |画像=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |所在地= {{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|蒲生郡|Q931658}}{{jalink|日野町 (滋賀県)|Q1208020|日野町}}村井705 |緯度度=35 |緯度分 =0 |緯度秒 =53.6 |経度度=136 |経度分 =15 |経度秒 =42.6 |地図=Japan Shiga |ラベル位置=bottom |祭神= {{jalink|天穂日命}}
{{jalink|天夷鳥命}}
武三熊大人命 |神体= |社格= {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建= （伝）{{jalink|欽明天皇|Q329792}}6年（{{jalink|545年|Q32860}}） |本殿= 三間社{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}} |別名= |札所等= |例祭= {{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}（毎年{{jalink|5月2日|Q2545|5月2}} - {{jalink|5月4日|Q2549|4日}}） |神事= }} '''馬見岡綿向神社'''（うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|蒲生郡|Q931658}}{{jalink|日野町 (滋賀県)|Q1208020|日野町}}の東にそびえる{{jalink|綿向山|Q11607423}}の山頂に祀られている大嵩神社の里宮。{{jalink|式内社}}馬見岡神社に比定{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=294}}。{{jalink|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。毎年5月2日と3日に例祭として{{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}を開催する。 == 祭神 == 綿向山を神体山とし、出雲系の三柱を祀る{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|pp=503-504}}{{Efn|神社の周囲は出雲系の人が多く住んでいたとされ、稲作の水源となる用水路も「出雲川」と呼ばれている{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。}}。 * {{jalink|天穂日命}}（アメノホヒノミコト） * {{jalink|天夷鳥命}}（アメノヒナドリノミコト） * 武三熊大人命（タケミクマオウシノミコト） == 歴史 == {{jalink|神武天皇|Q200188}}の時代に{{jalink|出雲国|Q582846}}の開拓神を迎えて祀り、545年（欽明天皇6年）に{{jalink|綿向山|Q11607423}}の頂上に祠を建てたのが始りと伝えられ、平安時代の初期の796年（延暦15年）に現在の地に移転されたとされている{{Cite web|和書|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=馬見岡綿向神社 - 近畿エリア {{!}} おでかけガイド：JRおでかけネット |publisher=西日本旅客鉄道 |accessdate=2018年3月7日}}。中世の1209年（承元3年）に境内の社殿がすべて焼失する火事があったが、社殿が再建されたのは181年後の1390年（明徳元年）のことであった{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2009|p=164}}。 ===蒲生氏の庇護=== 中世後期には日野に城下町を築いた{{jalink|蒲生氏|Q2975251}}が氏神として庇護し、蒲生上郡の総社として崇敬を集めた。1521年（大永2年）に兵火で焼けた社殿は、1549年（天文18年）に{{jalink|蒲生定秀|Q5368466}}、1571年（元亀2年）{{jalink|蒲生賢秀|Q842016}}によって修復された{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=298}}。また蒲生定秀は1557年（弘治3年）に神輿3基を新調し、蒲生氏が支配する領域128村の奉幣や地侍を従えて祭りの行列に参加した{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。{{jalink|蒲生氏郷|Q906580}}は1584年（天正12年）に伊勢に、その後会津に移ったが、当神社への寄進は続けていた{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。会津黒川に転封した際、この神社の参道一帯にあった「若松の森」と呼ばれた松林を偲び、新たな領地「会津黒川」を「{{jalink|会津若松}}」に改名したという。氏郷のあと蒲生氏は{{jalink|蒲生秀行 (侍従)|Q5520435|秀行}}・{{jalink|蒲生忠郷|Q8513818|忠郷}}・{{jalink|蒲生忠知|Q8513824|忠知}}と引き継がれてゆき、領地も会津の後は宇都宮・会津・松山と転封するが、綿向神社への寄進は継続していた{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。しかし1634年（寛永11年）当時伊予松山藩主であった{{jalink|蒲生忠知|Q8513824}}が嗣子なく没して蒲生家は残絶したため、蒲生氏からの庇護はなくなった{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|pp=298-299}}。 === 近江日野商人の信仰を集める === 近世には{{jalink|近江日野商人}}（{{jalink|近江商人|Q11638475}}）が出世開運の神として崇め、近江日野商人から寄進された拝殿・絵馬殿・石灯籠・石橋などが境内に残る{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=馬見岡綿向神社 |publisher =日野観光協会 |accessdate=2022-03-11}}。{{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}の際に神輿が渡御する半円形の石橋は1681年（延宝9年）に近江日野商人からの寄進で完成した{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|pp=433-434}}。1708年（宝永5年）現在の本殿が広く民衆からの募金により完成した{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|p=377}}。日野の町人中井源左衛門家の良祐・光昌親子は特に信仰が厚く、1782年（天明2年）神楽所を修復、1803年（享和3年）には拝殿を完成させた{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|pp=299-300}}。また社殿以外にも日野祭の当日に祭礼を彩る曳山19基（現在残っているのは16基）が、18世紀の初頭から日野町人たちの財力によって完成している{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。 === 朝香宮鳩彦王の宿舎 === 皇族の{{jalink|朝香宮鳩彦王|Q701503}}が1929年（昭和4年）10月に軍務で日野町を訪問することになった際に、社務所が宿舎に選定された。しかし、社務所は竣工したばかりで浴場の設備がなく、また塀垣の建造などが必要となったため、「殿下御専用の浴場」などの整備が行われた{{Sfn|滋賀県日野町教育会|1930|p=245}}。 朝香宮は10月24日午後3時、到着直後に当社を参拝した。25日には{{jalink|日野町立日野小学校|Q21654310|日野小学校}}、{{jalink|滋賀県立日野高等学校|Q11564656|日野高等女学校}}への御成の後、社務所前に記念樹として松を手植え、26日朝に出立した{{Sfn|滋賀県日野町教育会|1930|p=245}}。 ==境内== * 本殿 … 1998年滋賀県指定文化財{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|p=379}} * 拝殿 * 回廊 * 末社 ** 八幡宮 ** 村井御前社 ** 榊御前社 ** 竈殿 ** 富士権現社 ** 玉霊明神社 ** 大将軍社 ** 稲荷社 *手水舎 *神楽殿 *絵馬殿 *神馬舎 *社務所 *神輿庫 *石造灯篭 … 1942年国の重要美術品に認定{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|pp=153-155}} *石造反橋 == ギャラリー == 馬見岡綿向神社境内06.jpg|本殿 馬見岡綿向神社境内05.jpg|拝殿 Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|本殿横の猪 馬見岡綿向神社境内02.jpg|入り口の勧請縄 馬見岡綿向神社境内04.jpg|太鼓橋 馬見岡綿向神社境内01.jpg|伝若松の森跡 ==文化財== ===国認定重要美術品=== *馬見岡綿向神社石造灯篭 === 県指定有形文化財 === * 馬見岡綿向神社本殿一棟 * 祭礼渡御図絵馬{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=馬見岡綿向神社 |publisher =日野町 |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === 県指定無形民俗文化財 === * {{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}} == 現地情報 == '''所在地''' * {{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|蒲生郡|Q931658}}{{jalink|日野町 (滋賀県)|Q1208020|日野町}}村井705 '''交通アクセス'''{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=馬見岡綿向神社　アクセス |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=馬見岡綿向神社}} 公共交通機関 * {{jalink|西日本旅客鉄道|Q502125|JR}}{{jalink|東海道本線|Q1190152}}（{{jalink|琵琶湖線|Q804135}}）および{{jalink|近江鉄道八日市線|Q7081107}}（万葉あかね線）{{jalink|近江八幡駅|Q1042782}}または{{jalink|近江鉄道本線|Q7081104}}（水口・蒲生野線）{{jalink|日野駅 (滋賀県)|Q5767373|日野駅}}から{{jalink|近江鉄道バス|Q129172682}}北畑口行きで向町停留所下車、徒歩約3分。 自動車 * {{jalink|名神高速道路|Q904481}} {{jalink|八日市IC}}から約20分。 * {{jalink|新名神高速道路|Q7497118}} {{jalink|甲賀土山IC}}から約20分。 == 脚注 == === 注釈 {{nihongo|'''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine'''|馬見岡綿向神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Ōmi Province]].

{{wikidata link|Q24865035}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称= 馬見岡綿向神社 |画像=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |所在地= {{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|蒲生郡|Q931658}}{{jalink|日野町 (滋賀県)|Q1208020|日野町}}村井705 |緯度度=35 |緯度分 =0 |緯度秒 =53.6 |経度度=136 |経度分 =15 |経度秒 =42.6 |地図=Japan Shiga |ラベル位置=bottom |祭神= {{jalink|天穂日命}}
{{jalink|天夷鳥命}}
武三熊大人命 |神体= |社格= {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建= （伝）{{jalink|欽明天皇|Q329792}}6年（{{jalink|545年|Q32860}}） |本殿= 三間社{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}} |別名= |札所等= |例祭= {{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}（毎年{{jalink|5月2日|Q2545|5月2}} - {{jalink|5月4日|Q2549|4日}}） |神事= }} '''馬見岡綿向神社'''（うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|蒲生郡|Q931658}}{{jalink|日野町 (滋賀県)|Q1208020|日野町}}の東にそびえる{{jalink|綿向山|Q11607423}}の山頂に祀られている大嵩神社の里宮。{{jalink|式内社}}馬見岡神社に比定{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=294}}。{{jalink|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。毎年5月2日と3日に例祭として{{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}を開催する。 === 祭神 === 綿向山を神体山とし、出雲系の三柱を祀る{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|pp=503-504}}{{Efn|神社の周囲は出雲系の人が多く住んでいたとされ、稲作の水源となる用水路も「出雲川」と呼ばれている{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。}}。 * {{jalink|天穂日命}}（アメノホヒノミコト） * {{jalink|天夷鳥命}}（アメノヒナドリノミコト） * 武三熊大人命（タケミクマオウシノミコト） === 歴史 === {{jalink|神武天皇|Q200188}}の時代に{{jalink|出雲国|Q582846}}の開拓神を迎えて祀り、545年（欽明天皇6年）に{{jalink|綿向山|Q11607423}}の頂上に祠を建てたのが始りと伝えられ、平安時代の初期の796年（延暦15年）に現在の地に移転されたとされている{{Cite web|和書|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=馬見岡綿向神社 - 近畿エリア {{!}} おでかけガイド：JRおでかけネット |publisher=西日本旅客鉄道 |accessdate=2018年3月7日}}。中世の1209年（承元3年）に境内の社殿がすべて焼失する火事があったが、社殿が再建されたのは181年後の1390年（明徳元年）のことであった{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2009|p=164}}。 ===蒲生氏の庇護=== 中世後期には日野に城下町を築いた{{jalink|蒲生氏|Q2975251}}が氏神として庇護し、蒲生上郡の総社として崇敬を集めた。1521年（大永2年）に兵火で焼けた社殿は、1549年（天文18年）に{{jalink|蒲生定秀|Q5368466}}、1571年（元亀2年）{{jalink|蒲生賢秀|Q842016}}によって修復された{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=298}}。また蒲生定秀は1557年（弘治3年）に神輿3基を新調し、蒲生氏が支配する領域128村の奉幣や地侍を従えて祭りの行列に参加した{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。{{jalink|蒲生氏郷|Q906580}}は1584年（天正12年）に伊勢に、その後会津に移ったが、当神社への寄進は続けていた{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。会津黒川に転封した際、この神社の参道一帯にあった「若松の森」と呼ばれた松林を偲び、新たな領地「会津黒川」を「{{jalink|会津若松}}」に改名したという。氏郷のあと蒲生氏は{{jalink|蒲生秀行 (侍従)|Q5520435|秀行}}・{{jalink|蒲生忠郷|Q8513818|忠郷}}・{{jalink|蒲生忠知|Q8513824|忠知}}と引き継がれてゆき、領地も会津の後は宇都宮・会津・松山と転封するが、綿向神社への寄進は継続していた{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。しかし1634年（寛永11年）当時伊予松山藩主であった{{jalink|蒲生忠知|Q8513824}}が嗣子なく没して蒲生家は残絶したため、蒲生氏からの庇護はなくなった{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|pp=298-299}}。 === 近江日野商人の信仰を集める === 近世には{{jalink|近江日野商人}}（{{jalink|近江商人|Q11638475}}）が出世開運の神として崇め、近江日野商人から寄進された拝殿・絵馬殿・石灯籠・石橋などが境内に残る{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=馬見岡綿向神社 |publisher =日野観光協会 |accessdate=2022-03-11}}。{{jalink|日野祭|Q111034300}}の際に神輿が渡御する半円形の石橋は1681年（延宝9年）に近江日野商人からの寄進で完成した{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|pp=433-434}}。1708年（宝永5年）現在の本殿が広く民衆からの募金により完成した{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|p=377}}。日野の町人中井源左衛門家の良祐・光昌親子は特に信仰が厚く、1782年（天明2年）神楽所を修復、1803年（享和3年）には拝殿を完成させた{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2013|pp=299-300}}。また社殿以外にも日野祭の当日に祭礼を彩る曳山19基（現在残っているのは16基）が、18世紀の初頭から日野町人たちの財力によって完成している{{Sfn|平凡社地方資料センター|1991|p=504}}。 === 朝香宮鳩彦王の宿舎 === 皇族の{{jalink|朝香宮鳩彦王|Q701503}}が1929年（昭和4年）10月に軍務で日野町を訪問することになった際に、社務所が宿舎に選定された。しかし、社務所は竣工したばかりで浴場の設備がなく、また塀垣の建造などが必要となったため、「殿下御専用の浴場」などの整備が行われた{{Sfn|滋賀県日野町教育会|1930|p=245}}。 朝香宮は10月24日午後3時、到着直後に当社を参拝した。25日には{{jalink|日野町立日野小学校|Q21654310|日野小学校}}、{{jalink|滋賀県立日野高等学校|Q11564656|日野高等女学校}}への御成の後、社務所前に記念樹として松を手植え、26日朝に出立した{{Sfn|滋賀県日野町教育会|1930|p=245}}。 ===境内=== * 本殿 … 1998年滋賀県指定文化財{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|p=379}} * 拝殿 * 回廊 * 末社 ** 八幡宮 ** 村井御前社 ** 榊御前社 ** 竈殿 ** 富士権現社 ** 玉霊明神社 ** 大将軍社 ** 稲荷社 *手水舎 *神楽殿 *絵馬殿 *神馬舎 *社務所 *神輿庫 *石造灯篭 … 1942年国の重要美術品に認定{{Sfn|日野町史編さん委員会|2007|pp=153-155}} *石造反橋 === ギャラリー === 馬見岡綿向神社境内06.jpg|本殿 馬見岡綿向神社境内05.jpg|拝殿 Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|本殿横の猪 馬見岡綿向神社境内02.jpg|入り口の勧請縄 馬見岡綿向神社境内04.jpg|太鼓橋 {{wikidata link|Q24865035}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |Image=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{jalink|545|Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} (Every year {{jalink|May 2|Q2545|May 2}}–{{jalink|May 4|Q2549|4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}}, {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{jalink|Gamō clan|Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{jalink|Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{jalink|Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{jalink|Gamō Ujisato|Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{jalink|Aizuwakamatsu}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{jalink|Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|Hideyuki}}, {{jalink|Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|Tadayoshi}}, and {{jalink|Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{jalink|Ōmi-Hino merchants}} ({{jalink|Ōmi merchants|Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === {{wikidata link|Q24865035}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |Image=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{jalink|545|Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} (Every year {{jalink|May 2|Q2545|May 2}}–{{jalink|May 4|Q2549|4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}}, {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{jalink|Gamō clan|Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{jalink|Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{jalink|Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{jalink|Gamō Ujisato|Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{jalink|Aizuwakamatsu}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{jalink|Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|Hideyuki}}, {{jalink|Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|Tadayoshi}}, and {{jalink|Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{jalink|Ōmi-Hino merchants}} ({{jalink|Ōmi merchants|Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall {{wikidata link|Q24865035}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |Image=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{jalink|545|Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} (Every year {{jalink|May 2|Q2545|May 2}}–{{jalink|May 4|Q2549|4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}}, {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{jalink|Gamō clan|Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{jalink|Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{jalink|Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{jalink|Gamō Ujisato|Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{jalink|Aizuwakamatsu}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{jalink|Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|Hideyuki}}, {{jalink|Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|Tadayoshi}}, and {{jalink|Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{jalink|Ōmi-Hino merchants}} ({{jalink|Ōmi merchants|Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|Boar beside the main hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 02.jpg|Inviting rope at the entrance Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 04.jpg|Drum bridge Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 01.jpg|Site of the legendary Wakamatsu Grove === Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Important Art Objects === * Stone Lantern of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine === Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine (one structure) * Festival Procession Illustrated Ema{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Town |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === Prefecturally Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} === On-site Information === '''Address''' * {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 '''Access'''{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Access |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine}} Public Transportation * From {{jalink|Ōmihachiman Station|Q1042782}} on the {{jalink|Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152}} ({{jalink|Biwako Line|Q804135}}) of {{jalink|West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|JR}} or the {{jalink|Ōmi Railway Yōkaichi Line|Q7081107}} (Manyō Akane Line), or from {{jalink|Hino Station (Shiga)|Q5767373|Hino Station}} on the {{jalink|Ōmi Railway Main Line|Q7081104}} (Minakuchi–Gamōno Line). Take the {{jalink|Ōmi Railway Bus|Q129172682}} bound for Kitahataguchi, get off at Mukōmachi stop, then walk about 3 minutes. By Car * About 20 minutes from {{jalink|Yōkaichi IC}} on the {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}. * About 20 minutes from {{jalink|Kōka-Tsuchiyama IC}} on the {{jalink|Shin-Meishin Expressway|Q7497118}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association |title=History of Ōmi Hino Town |volume=Middle Volume |publisher=Hino Town Education Association |year=1930 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 2 (Medieval Period) |publisher=Hino Town |year=2009-12 |id={{JP number|21712752}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 3 (Early Modern Period) |publisher=Hino Town |date=2013-03 |id={{JP number|22230365}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}}

Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{jalink|545|Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} (Every year {{jalink|May 2|Q2545|May 2}}–{{jalink|May 4|Q2549|4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}}, {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{jalink|Gamō clan|Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{jalink|Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{jalink|Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{jalink|Gamō Ujisato|Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{jalink|Aizuwakamatsu}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{jalink|Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|Hideyuki}}, {{jalink|Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|Tadayoshi}}, and {{jalink|Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{jalink|Ōmi-Hino merchants}} ({{jalink|Ōmi merchants|Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === {{wikidata link|Q24865035}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |Image=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}{{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{jalink|545|Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} (Every year {{jalink|May 2|Q2545|May 2}}–{{jalink|May 4|Q2549|4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{jalink|Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|Hino Town}}, {{jalink|Gamo District|Q931658}}, {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{jalink|Mount Watamuki|Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{jalink|Gamō clan|Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{jalink|Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{jalink|Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{jalink|Gamō Ujisato|Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{jalink|Aizuwakamatsu}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{jalink|Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|Hideyuki}}, {{jalink|Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|Tadayoshi}}, and {{jalink|Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{jalink|Ōmi-Hino merchants}} ({{jalink|Ōmi merchants|Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{jalink|Hino Festival|Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{jalink|Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{jalink|Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|Hino Elementary School}} and {{jalink|Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}
{{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill||lt=Aizuwakamatsu|UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill||lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}

Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial
