Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|Boar beside the main hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 02.jpg|Inviting rope at the entrance Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 04.jpg|Drum bridge Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 01.jpg|Site of the legendary Wakamatsu Grove === Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Important Art Objects === * Stone Lantern of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine === Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine (one structure) * Festival Procession Illustrated Ema{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Town |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === Prefecturally Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300}} === On-site Information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town}} Murai 705 '''Access'''{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Access |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine}} Public Transportation * From {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman Station|Q1042782|WD=Q1042782}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135}}) of {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR}} or the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Yōkaichi Line|Q7081107|WD=Q7081107}} (Manyō Akane Line), or from {{ill|lt=Hino Station (Shiga)|Q5767373|WD=Q5767373|lt=Hino Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Main Line|Q7081104|WD=Q7081104}} (Minakuchi–Gamōno Line). Take the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Bus|Q129172682|WD=Q129172682}} bound for Kitahataguchi, get off at Mukōmachi stop, then walk about 3 minutes. By Car * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yōkaichi IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Meishin Expressway|Q904481|WD=Q904481}}. * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōka-Tsuchiyama IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Shin-Meishin Expressway|Q7497118|WD=Q7497118}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860|1=545}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2|1=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4|1=May 4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860|1=545}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2|1=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4|1=May 4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|Boar beside the main hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 02.jpg|Inviting rope at the entrance Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 04.jpg|Drum bridge Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 01.jpg|Site of the legendary Wakamatsu Grove === Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Important Art Objects === * Stone Lantern of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine === Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine (one structure) * Festival Procession Illustrated Ema{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Town |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === Prefecturally Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} === On-site Information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}} Murai 705 '''Access'''{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Access |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine}} Public Transportation * From {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman Station|Q1042782|WD=Q1042782|1=Ōmi-Hachiman Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135|1=Biwako Line}}) of {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR|1=West Japan Railway Company}} or the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Yōkaichi Line|Q7081107|WD=Q7081107|1=Ohmi Railway Yōkaichi Line}} (Manyō Akane Line), or from {{ill|lt=Hino Station (Shiga)|Q5767373|WD=Q5767373|lt=Hino Station|1=Hino Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Main Line|Q7081104|WD=Q7081104|1=Ohmi Railway Main Line}} (Minakuchi–Gamōno Line). Take the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Bus|Q129172682|WD=Q129172682|1=Ohmi Railway Bus}} bound for Kitahataguchi, get off at Mukōmachi stop, then walk about 3 minutes. By Car * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yōkaichi IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Meishin Expressway|Q904481|WD=Q904481|1=Meishin Expressway}}. * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōka-Tsuchiyama IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Shin-Meishin Expressway|Q7497118|WD=Q7497118|1=Shin-Meishin Expressway}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|Boar beside the main hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 02.jpg|Inviting rope at the entrance Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 04.jpg|Drum bridge Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 01.jpg|Site of the legendary Wakamatsu Grove === Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Important Art Objects === * Stone Lantern of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine === Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine (one structure) * Festival Procession Illustrated Ema{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Town |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === Prefecturally Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} === On-site Information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}} Murai 705 '''Access'''{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Access |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine}} Public Transportation * From {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman Station|Q1042782|WD=Q1042782|1=Ōmi-Hachiman Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135|1=Biwako Line}}) of {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR|1=West Japan Railway Company}} or the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Yōkaichi Line|Q7081107|WD=Q7081107|1=Ohmi Railway Yōkaichi Line}} (Manyō Akane Line), or from {{ill|lt=Hino Station (Shiga)|Q5767373|WD=Q5767373|lt=Hino Station|1=Hino Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Main Line|Q7081104|WD=Q7081104|1=Ohmi Railway Main Line}} (Minakuchi–Gamōno Line). Take the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Bus|Q129172682|WD=Q129172682|1=Ohmi Railway Bus}} bound for Kitahataguchi, get off at Mukōmachi stop, then walk about 3 minutes. By Car * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yōkaichi IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Meishin Expressway|Q904481|WD=Q904481|1=Meishin Expressway}}. * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōka-Tsuchiyama IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Shin-Meishin Expressway|Q7497118|WD=Q7497118|1=Shin-Meishin Expressway}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association |title=History of Ōmi Hino Town |volume=Middle Volume |publisher=Hino Town Education Association |year=1930 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 2 (Medieval Period) |publisher=Hino Town |year=2009-12 |id={{JP number|21712752}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 3 (Early Modern Period) |publisher=Hino Town |date=2013-03 |id={{JP number|22230365}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 5 (Cultural Properties) |publisher=Hino Town |date=2007-03 |id={{JP number|21550149}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Heibonsha Regional Data Center |title={{ill|lt=Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Place Names|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} |volume=Vol. 25 (Place Names of Shiga Prefecture) |publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}} |date=1991-02 |isbn=4-582-49025-5 |ref=harv}} * “Brief History of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine” – Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Office * “Want Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860|1=545}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2|1=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4|1=May 4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860|1=545}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2|1=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4|1=May 4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as Important Art Objects of the Nation in 1942 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=153–155}} * Stone Arched Bridge === Gallery === Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 06.jpg|Main Hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 05.jpg|Worship Hall Umamiokawatanukizinnzyainosisi.JPG|Boar beside the main hall Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 02.jpg|Inviting rope at the entrance Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 04.jpg|Drum bridge Umamioka Watamuki Shrine precincts 01.jpg|Site of the legendary Wakamatsu Grove === Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Important Art Objects === * Stone Lantern of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine === Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine (one structure) * Festival Procession Illustrated Ema{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.town.shiga-hino.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?co=kak&frmId=4888 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Town |accessdate=2022-03-11}} === Prefecturally Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} === On-site Information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}} Murai 705 '''Access'''{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://www.ex.biwa.ne.jp/~j-watamuki/access.html |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Access |accessdate=2022-03-02 |publisher=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine}} Public Transportation * From {{ill|lt=Ōmihachiman Station|Q1042782|WD=Q1042782|1=Ōmi-Hachiman Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} ({{ill|lt=Biwako Line|Q804135|WD=Q804135|1=Biwako Line}}) of {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR|1=West Japan Railway Company}} or the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Yōkaichi Line|Q7081107|WD=Q7081107|1=Ohmi Railway Yōkaichi Line}} (Manyō Akane Line), or from {{ill|lt=Hino Station (Shiga)|Q5767373|WD=Q5767373|lt=Hino Station|1=Hino Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Main Line|Q7081104|WD=Q7081104|1=Ohmi Railway Main Line}} (Minakuchi–Gamōno Line). Take the {{ill|lt=Ōmi Railway Bus|Q129172682|WD=Q129172682|1=Ohmi Railway Bus}} bound for Kitahataguchi, get off at Mukōmachi stop, then walk about 3 minutes. By Car * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yōkaichi IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Meishin Expressway|Q904481|WD=Q904481|1=Meishin Expressway}}. * About 20 minutes from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōka-Tsuchiyama IC|1=UNKNOWN}} on the {{ill|lt=Shin-Meishin Expressway|Q7497118|WD=Q7497118|1=Shin-Meishin Expressway}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association |title=History of Ōmi Hino Town |volume=Middle Volume |publisher=Hino Town Education Association |year=1930 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 2 (Medieval Period) |publisher=Hino Town |year=2009-12 |id={{JP number|21712752}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 3 (Early Modern Period) |publisher=Hino Town |date=2013-03 |id={{JP number|22230365}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Hino Town History Editorial Committee |title=History of Ōmi Hino |volume=Vol. 5 (Cultural Properties) |publisher=Hino Town |date=2007-03 |id={{JP number|21550149}} |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Heibonsha Regional Data Center |title={{ill|lt=Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Place Names|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} |volume=Vol. 25 (Place Names of Shiga Prefecture) |publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}} |date=1991-02 |isbn=4-582-49025-5 |ref=harv}} * “Brief History of Umamioka Watamuki Shrine” – Umamioka Watamuki Shrine Office * “Want to Know More About Hino” – Hino Tourism Volunteer Association === Related Items === * {{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}} === External Links === * {{Official
