Shrine |Image=[[File:Umamiokawatanukizinnzyahaiden.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}} Murai 705 |Latitude Degrees=35 |Latitude Minutes=0 |Latitude Seconds=53.6 |Longitude Degrees=136 |Longitude Minutes=15 |Longitude Seconds=42.6 |Map=Japan Shiga |Label Position=bottom |Enshrined Deities= {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto |Sacred Object= |Shrine Rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded= (Trad.) Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=545|Q32860|WD=Q32860|1=545}}) |Main Hall= Three-bay shrine, {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternate Name= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Annual Festival= {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} (Every year {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545|lt=May 2|1=May 2}}–{{ill|lt=May 4|Q2549|WD=Q2549|lt=4|1=May 4}}) |Rituals= }} '''Umamioka Watamuki Shrine''' (うまみおかわたむきじんじゃ) is the satomiya (village shrine) of Ōtake Shrine, which is enshrined on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}} towering to the east of {{ill|lt=Hino Town (Shiga)|Q1208020|WD=Q1208020|lt=Hino Town|1=Hino, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Gamo District|Q931658|WD=Q931658|1=Gamō District, Shiga}}, {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. It is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} Umamioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=294}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Every year on May 2 and 3, the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} is held as the annual festival. === Enshrined Deities === Mount Watamuki is regarded as the sacred mountain, and three Izumo-related deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|pp=503–504}}{{Efn|It is said that many people of Izumo lineage lived around the shrine, and the irrigation canal that serves as the water source for rice cultivation is also called the “Izumo River.” {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame no Hohi}} (Amenohohi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hinadore-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Amenohinadore-no-Mikoto) * Takemikumao-ushi-no-Mikoto (Takemikumao-ōshi-no-Mikoto) === History === According to tradition, during the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a pioneer deity from {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}} was invited and enshrined, and in 545 (Year 6 of Emperor Kinmei), a shrine was built on the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Watamuki|Q11607423|WD=Q11607423|1=Mount Watamuki}}, marking the beginning of the shrine. In 796 (Year 15 of Enryaku) in the early Heian period, it is said to have been relocated to its present site.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://guide.jr-odekake.net/spot/1003 |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine - Kinki Area | Odekake Guide: JR Odekake Net |publisher=West Japan Railway Company |accessdate=March 7, 2018}} In the medieval period, in 1209 (Year 3 of Shōgen), a fire destroyed all the shrine buildings, but reconstruction did not occur until 181 years later, in 1390 (Year 1 of Meitoku) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2009|p=164}}. === Patronage of the Gamō Clan === In the late medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Gamō clan|Q2975251|WD=Q2975251|1=Gamō clan}}, who built a castle town in Hino, patronized the shrine as their clan shrine, and it gained reverence as the general shrine of Upper Gamō District. The shrine buildings, which had burned down in warfare in 1521 (Year 2 of Daiei), were repaired in 1549 (Year 18 of Tenmon) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Sadahide|Q5368466|WD=Q5368466|1=Gamō Sadahide}} and in 1571 (Year 2 of Genki) by {{ill|lt=Gamō Yoshihide|Q842016|WD=Q842016|1=Gamō Katahide}} {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=298}}. In addition, in 1557 (Year 3 of Kōji), Gamō Sadahide commissioned three new mikoshi (portable shrines) and participated in the festival procession accompanied by offerings and local samurai from 128 villages under Gamō control {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. {{ill|lt=Gamō Ujisato|Q906580|WD=Q906580|1=Gamō Ujisato}} moved to Ise in 1584 (Year 12 of Tenshō) and later to Aizu, but continued making donations to this shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. When he was transferred to Aizu-Kurokawa, he is said to have renamed the new domain “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|1=UNKNOWN}}” in memory of the pine grove called “Wakamatsu no Mori” that once stood along the approach to this shrine. After Ujisato, the Gamō lineage continued through {{ill|lt=Gamō Hideyuki (courtier)|Q5520435|WD=Q5520435|lt=Hideyuki|1=Gamō Hideyuki}}, {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadayoshi|Q8513818|WD=Q8513818|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Gamō Tadasato}}, and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadachika|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824|lt=Tadachika|1=Gamō Tadatomo}}, and although their domains were transferred successively from Aizu to Utsunomiya, Aizu, and Matsuyama, donations to Watamuki Shrine continued {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. However, in 1634 (Year 11 of Kan’ei), Gamō Tadachika, then lord of Iyo Matsuyama Domain, died without an heir, leading to the extinction of the Gamō family, and patronage from the clan ceased {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=298–299}}. === Worship by Ōmi-Hino Merchants === In the early modern period, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōmi-Hino merchants|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Ōmi merchants|Q11638475|WD=Q11638475|1=Ōmi merchant}}) revered the shrine as a deity of success and good fortune. The worship hall, ema hall, stone lanterns, stone bridges, and other structures donated by these merchants remain within the precincts.{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.hino-kanko.jp/sight/umamioka/ |title=Umamioka Watamuki Shrine |publisher=Hino Tourism Association |accessdate=2022-03-11}} The semicircular stone bridge over which the mikoshi pass during the {{ill|lt=Hino Festival|Q111034300|WD=Q111034300|1=Hino Matsuri}} was completed in 1681 (Year 9 of Enpō) through donations from Ōmi-Hino merchants {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|pp=433–434}}. In 1708 (Year 5 of Hōei), the current main hall was completed through widespread public fundraising {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|p=299}}{{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=377}}. The Nakaigenzaemon family of Hino townspeople, particularly the father-and-son pair Ryōyū and Mitsumasa, were especially devoted; they repaired the kagura hall in 1782 (Year 2 of Tenmei) and completed the worship hall in 1803 (Year 3 of Kyōwa) {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2013|pp=299–300}}. In addition to the shrine buildings, 19 festival floats (16 of which remain today) that adorn the Hino Festival were completed from the early 18th century through the financial power of Hino townspeople {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1991|p=504}}. === Lodging of Prince Asaka Yasuhiko === When the imperial family member {{ill|lt=Prince Asaka Yasuhiko|Q701503|WD=Q701503|1=Prince Yasuhiko Asaka}} was scheduled to visit Hino Town on military duty in October 1929 (Shōwa 4), the shrine office was selected as his lodging. However, since the office had just been completed and lacked bathing facilities, and construction of fences and other features was necessary, facilities such as a “bath exclusively for His Highness” were newly installed {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. Prince Asaka arrived at 3:00 p.m. on October 24 and immediately paid a visit to the shrine. On the 25th, after visits to {{ill|lt=Hino Municipal Hino Elementary School|Q21654310|WD=Q21654310|lt=Hino Elementary School|1=Hino Elementary School}} and {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefectural Hino High School|Q11564656|WD=Q11564656|lt=Hino Girls’ High School|1=Hino Girls’ High School}}, he personally planted a pine tree in front of the shrine office as a commemorative tree, and departed on the morning of the 26th {{Sfn|Hino Town Shiga Prefecture Education Association|1930|p=245}}. === Shrine Precincts === * Main Hall … Designated Shiga Prefecture Cultural Property in 1998 {{Sfn|Hino Town History Editorial Committee|2007|p=379}} * Worship Hall * Corridor * Subsidiary Shrines ** Hachiman Shrine ** Murai Gozen Shrine ** Sakaki Gozen Shrine ** Kamado Hall ** Fuji Gongen Shrine ** Gyōrei Myōjin Shrine ** Daishōgun Shrine ** Inari Shrine * Temizuya (Purification Pavilion) * Kagura Hall * Ema Hall * Sacred Horse Stable * Shrine Office * Mikoshi Storehouse * Stone Lanterns … Certified as
